subtopic_detail: KDIGO eGFR/ACR matrix + KHA-CARI referral thresholds
stem: A 64-year-old man attends his GP for a chronic disease check. He has type 2 diabetes for 12 years, hypertension, and a BMI of 33 kg/m^2. Medications include perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, empagliflozin 10 mg, and atorvastatin 40 mg. He denies haematuria, frothy urine, oedema or new urinary symptoms and has had no recent illness, NSAIDs or contrast exposure. He has never been seen by a nephrologist. Examination shows BP 142/86 mmHg, no peripheral oedema, normal heart sounds and chest. Fundi show background diabetic retinopathy. Today's results in stable conditions: serum creatinine 168 micromol/L (was 162 three months ago and 158 six months ago); eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2; urine albumin to creatinine ratio (ACR) on a first-morning sample is 42 mg/mmol, confirmed on a repeat sample two weeks later at 38 mg/mmol; serum potassium 4.6 mmol/L; HbA1c 58 mmol/mol; haemoglobin 128 g/L. He has had no episodes of AKI in the past year.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 142/86 mmHg, HR 76 bpm, BMI 33 kg/m^2.
data_table.Examination: No peripheral oedema, JVP not raised, fundi: background diabetic retinopathy.
data_table.Investigations: Creatinine 168 micromol/L (stable), eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2, urine ACR 42 mg/mmol (confirmed 38 mg/mmol on repeat), K 4.6 mmol/L, HbA1c 58 mmol/mol, Hb 128 g/L.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, empagliflozin 10 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in his renal management?

options.text: Continue GP-led care and recheck eGFR and ACR in 6 months without specialist input
options.rationale: KHA-CARI and Kidney Health Australia advise referral when eGFR is below 30 mL/min/1.73m^2, when ACR is above 30 mg/mmol regardless of eGFR, or when there is an unexplained rapid decline; this patient meets the ACR criterion so GP-only care is below standard (Source: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024).
source_refs: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024

options.text: Refer only if eGFR falls below 30 mL/min/1.73m^2 on the next test
options.rationale: Australian guidance lists ACR persistently above 30 mg/mmol as an independent referral trigger; waiting for eGFR to fall under 30 delays specialist input on renoprotection and dialysis planning (Source: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024).
source_refs: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024

options.text: Stop empagliflozin because the eGFR has fallen below 45 mL/min/1.73m^2
options.rationale: SGLT2 inhibitors are continued for cardiorenal protection down to dialysis in CKD; empagliflozin is initiated and continued at eGFR thresholds well below 45 in the Australian PBS criteria for cardiorenal indications (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - SGLT2 inhibitors).

options.text: Stop perindopril given the modest creatinine rise and switch to amlodipine
options.rationale: ACE inhibitors are renoprotective and should be continued unless creatinine rises by more than 30 percent or potassium exceeds 6.0 mmol/L; his creatinine is stable and potassium is 4.6 mmol/L (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - hypertension in CKD).
source_refs: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - hypertension in CKD

options.text: Refer to nephrology for assessment given persistent ACR above 30 mg/mmol
options.rationale: He has CKD stage G3b A3 (eGFR 30-44, ACR above 30 mg/mmol). Australian primary care guidance is to refer to nephrology when ACR is persistently above 30 mg/mmol or eGFR is below 30, both as standalone triggers, to optimise renoprotection and plan for advanced CKD (Source: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024).
source_refs: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024

explanation.summary: On the KDIGO heatmap his stage is G3b A3, an Australian referral trigger by ACR alone. Continue ACE inhibitor and SGLT2 inhibitor, optimise BP and HbA1c, and refer to nephrology (Source: KHA-CARI 2024).
key_points: Refer if eGFR < 30, ACR > 30 mg/mmol, or rapid decline (KHA-CARI)
G3b A3 carries high CV and ESKD risk - colour red on KDIGO heatmap
Continue ACEi unless Cr rises > 30% or K+ > 6.0 mmol/L
Continue SGLT2i for cardiorenal benefit down to dialysis
explanation.pearls: ACR > 30 mg/mmol alone is enough - don't wait for eGFR to drop.

sources.label: KHA-CARI / Kidney Health Australia CKD Management in Primary Care 2024
Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular - hypertension in CKD

subtopic_detail: Iron repletion before ESA, KDIGO Hb thresholds
stem: A 72-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2, secondary to long-standing hypertension and obstructive uropathy after pelvic radiotherapy) is seen in renal outpatients. She reports six weeks of fatigue and exertional dyspnoea climbing one flight of stairs. She denies overt bleeding, melaena, haematuria or menorrhagia (post-menopausal). Medications: amlodipine 10 mg, irbesartan 300 mg, atorvastatin 40 mg, cholecalciferol 1000 IU. She does not take NSAIDs. There has been no recent infection or hospitalisation. Examination: BP 138/82, conjunctival pallor, mild ankle oedema, otherwise unremarkable. Bloods (today): Hb 96 g/L, MCV 88 fL, ferritin 78 microgram/L, transferrin saturation 14 percent, CRP 4 mg/L, B12 and folate normal, reticulocyte count low, creatinine 232 micromol/L (stable), eGFR 22, K 4.4 mmol/L, PTH 18 pmol/L, corrected Ca 2.30 mmol/L, phosphate 1.4 mmol/L. Faecal occult blood is negative on three samples.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, SpO2 97 percent on room air.
data_table.Examination: Conjunctival pallor, mild ankle oedema. JVP not raised. No abdominal mass; PR exam unremarkable.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 96 g/L, MCV 88, ferritin 78 microgram/L, TSAT 14%, CRP 4 mg/L, retics low, eGFR 22, PTH 18 pmol/L, Ca 2.30, PO4 1.4.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in managing her anaemia?

options.text: Commence subcutaneous darbepoetin alfa and review Hb in 4 weeks
options.rationale: Australian and KDIGO guidance requires correction of iron deficiency before starting an erythropoiesis-stimulating agent. Her transferrin saturation 14% indicates functional iron deficiency, and starting an ESA without repleting iron risks an inadequate response and higher ESA doses (Source: KDIGO Anaemia in CKD 2012 / 2024 update; KHA-CARI Anaemia 2023).

options.text: Give IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) targeting TSAT 20-30% and ferritin 100-500, then reassess Hb
options.rationale: She has functional iron deficiency (TSAT 14%, ferritin within absolute-deficiency range for CKD) and Hb 96 g/L. KHA-CARI and KDIGO recommend a trial of IV iron in non-dialysis CKD to target TSAT > 20-30% and ferritin > 100 microgram/L before considering an ESA, since many patients respond to iron alone (Source: KHA-CARI Anaemia 2023; KDIGO 2012/2024).

options.text: Trial oral ferrous sulfate 325 mg daily and recheck iron studies in 3 months
options.rationale: Oral iron is poorly absorbed and tolerated in CKD stage 4-5, particularly in patients on ACEi/ARB and with elevated hepcidin; IV iron is preferred in non-dialysis CKD with TSAT below 30% and ferritin below 500 (Source: KHA-CARI Anaemia of CKD 2023).

options.text: Transfuse 2 units of packed red cells today and arrange outpatient ESA
options.rationale: Hb 96 g/L without active bleeding or symptoms of acute decompensation does not meet transfusion thresholds; transfusion in CKD also sensitises potential transplant recipients (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management Module 3 - Medical).

options.text: Investigate for occult GI malignancy with colonoscopy before any treatment
options.rationale: Her pattern fits anaemia of CKD with functional iron deficiency; FOBT is negative and there is no GI symptomatology. GI workup is reserved for unexplained iron deficiency or red flags, not the first step here (Source: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Screening 2023).
source_refs: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Screening 2023

explanation.summary: Iron deficiency must be corrected before ESA in CKD anaemia. IV iron is preferred in non-dialysis CKD when TSAT < 30% or ferritin < 500. ESAs are added only if Hb remains < 100 g/L after iron repletion, targeting 100-115 g/L (Source: KHA-CARI 2023).
key_points: Target TSAT > 20-30% and ferritin > 100 microgram/L before ESA
IV iron preferred over oral in non-dialysis CKD stage 4-5
ESA target Hb 100-115 g/L - avoid > 115 due to CV harm signal (TREAT, CHOIR)
Always exclude bleeding, haemolysis, B12/folate deficiency
explanation.pearls: Functional iron deficiency = low TSAT despite normal/raised ferritin; hepcidin-mediated.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia Colorectal Cancer Screening 2023

subtopic_detail: Sequencing phosphate binder vs activated vitamin D vs cinacalcet
stem: A 58-year-old man with CKD stage 5 not yet on dialysis (eGFR 12 mL/min/1.73m^2 due to ADPKD) is reviewed in nephrology clinic. He is asymptomatic of bone pain or pruritus but has cramping. He follows a low-phosphate diet with the renal dietitian. Medications: amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, sodium bicarbonate 840 mg TDS, cholecalciferol 1000 IU. He is not yet anuric. Bloods at today's visit (consistent with the trend over the last 3 months): corrected calcium 2.18 mmol/L, phosphate 1.95 mmol/L (rising from 1.5 three months ago), PTH 78 pmol/L (rising from 45), 25-OH vitamin D 78 nmol/L, alkaline phosphatase 142 U/L, bicarbonate 22 mmol/L. He is engaged in dialysis education and a fistula has been formed. He is being worked up for kidney transplantation.

data_table.Examination: No bone tenderness; mature radiocephalic AV fistula on left forearm with good thrill.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 2.18 mmol/L, phosphate 1.95 mmol/L (rising), PTH 78 pmol/L (rising), 25-OH vit D 78 nmol/L, ALP 142 U/L, HCO3 22, eGFR 12.
data_table.Medications: Amlodipine, irbesartan, atorvastatin, NaHCO3 840 mg TDS, cholecalciferol 1000 IU.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in managing his CKD-MBD?

options.text: Commence oral calcium carbonate 600 mg with each meal as a phosphate binder
options.rationale: Calcium-containing binders add to overall calcium load and are associated with greater progression of vascular calcification in CKD; KDIGO 2017 recommends restricting their use, particularly when other binders are available (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).

options.text: Start oral calcitriol 0.25 microgram daily to suppress the rising PTH
options.rationale: Activated vitamin D analogues raise both calcium and phosphate via gut absorption; starting calcitriol with phosphate 1.95 mmol/L will worsen hyperphosphataemia and accelerate vascular calcification. Phosphate control must come first (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017).

options.text: Commence oral cinacalcet 30 mg daily as a calcimimetic
options.rationale: Cinacalcet (a calcimimetic) is PBS-restricted to dialysis patients in Australia and lowers calcium; with corrected calcium already 2.18 mmol/L hypocalcaemia is a risk. It is not first-line in non-dialysis CKD (Source: PBS - cinacalcet; AMH).

options.text: Add a non-calcium phosphate binder (sevelamer) and reinforce dietary phosphate restriction
options.rationale: KDIGO and KHA-CARI advise lowering phosphate towards the normal range as the first lever in CKD 5 with rising phosphate and PTH. A non-calcium binder is preferred over calcium-based binders because of vascular calcification risk, particularly with a calcium near the lower limit (Source: KDIGO CKD-MBD 2017 Update; KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

options.text: Refer urgently for subtotal parathyroidectomy given the rising PTH
options.rationale: Parathyroidectomy is reserved for severe medically refractory secondary hyperparathyroidism, typically PTH persistently above approximately 88 pmol/L (800 pg/mL) despite optimal medical therapy with hypercalcaemia/hyperphosphataemia or symptomatic disease; medical therapy has not yet been escalated (Source: KHA-CARI CKD-MBD).

explanation.summary: In CKD-MBD, control phosphate first using diet plus a non-calcium binder, then address PTH with activated vitamin D once phosphate is acceptable. Avoid loading calcium and avoid activated vitamin D while phosphate is high (Source: KDIGO 2017).
key_points: Phosphate first, PTH second in CKD-MBD
Non-calcium binders preferred (sevelamer, lanthanum)
Activated vitamin D raises phosphate - sequence after phosphate control
Cinacalcet restricted to dialysis in Australia (PBS)
explanation.pearls: Rising PTH with rising phosphate = give a binder before reaching for calcitriol.

subtopic_detail: AEIOU + advance care planning in frail older adult
stem: An 82-year-old woman with CKD stage 5 (eGFR 8 mL/min/1.73m^2 due to renovascular disease and longstanding T2DM) is admitted with fatigue, anorexia and one week of intermittent confusion. She lives alone with package supports, has a Clinical Frailty Scale of 7, and a Charlson index of 9 (T2DM with retinopathy and neuropathy, IHD with PCI, HF with EF 35 percent, COPD). She has been counselled previously about home haemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis and has expressed a preference 'not to live on a machine' but has not formalised this. She is alert, oriented to person and place only. Bloods: K 5.4 mmol/L, HCO3 19 mmol/L, urea 38 mmol/L, creatinine 412 micromol/L, Hb 92 g/L, no acidaemia on VBG (pH 7.34), no pulmonary oedema on CXR, urine output 1100 mL/day. She has not been exposed to NSAIDs, contrast or new nephrotoxins.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, RR 18, SpO2 96 percent on RA, T 36.7.
data_table.Examination: Mild asterixis, mild peripheral oedema, no pericardial rub. Clear chest.
data_table.Investigations: K 5.4, HCO3 19, urea 38 mmol/L, creatinine 412 micromol/L, Hb 92, pH 7.34, normal CXR, UO 1100 mL/day.
data_table.Medications: Frusemide 40 mg, amlodipine, aspirin, metoprolol, atorvastatin, gliclazide MR 60 mg, insulin glargine 14 units.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Insert a tunnelled vascath and commence urgent in-centre haemodialysis today
options.rationale: Her bloods do not meet AEIOU criteria for emergency dialysis (no severe acidosis, no refractory hyperkalaemia, no fluid overload, no overt uraemic encephalopathy/pericarditis, no toxic ingestion); urgent dialysis bypasses her stated preferences (Source: KHA-CARI Dialysis 2024; ANZSN Renal Supportive Care).

options.text: Hold a formal goals-of-care conversation and offer renal supportive (conservative) care
options.rationale: In a frail older adult (CFS 7, Charlson 9) without acute AEIOU indications, evidence from Australian cohorts and the ANZSN Renal Supportive Care framework shows dialysis offers limited survival or quality-of-life gain over conservative care. The next step is to formalise her preferences and offer a structured conservative pathway with symptom-focused support (Source: ANZSN Renal Supportive Care 2022; KHA Conservative Pathway 2023).

options.text: Commence inpatient peritoneal dialysis training and discharge home on PD
options.rationale: Implementing PD without first revisiting her stated preference 'not to live on a machine' is not patient-centred; PD requires significant manual dexterity and home support that her CFS 7 status may not allow (Source: ANZSN Renal Supportive Care 2022).

options.text: Insert an indwelling urinary catheter for accurate urine output then discharge with GP review
options.rationale: Catheterisation does not address her uraemic symptoms or the need for a values-based conversation, and it adds CAUTI risk; her urine output is already documented (Source: ANZSN Renal Supportive Care 2022).

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit of packed red cells targeting Hb 110 g/L to relieve her fatigue
options.rationale: Hb 92 g/L without bleeding or haemodynamic compromise does not meet transfusion thresholds and risks fluid overload and transplant sensitisation; iron studies and ESA optimisation are the standard pathway (Source: NBA Patient Blood Management Module 3).

explanation.summary: Dialysis decisions integrate AEIOU criteria, frailty (Clinical Frailty Scale, Charlson index), and patient values. In frail older adults without absolute AEIOU indications, a structured renal supportive care pathway often matches or exceeds dialysis on quality-adjusted outcomes (Source: ANZSN 2022).
key_points: AEIOU: Acidosis, Electrolytes (refractory K), Ingestion (toxin), Overload, Uraemia (pericarditis/encephalopathy)
CFS 7 + Charlson 9 = frail; dialysis survival benefit limited
Document an advance care directive in NSW/Vic statutory form
Renal Supportive Care is an active treatment, not 'nothing'
explanation.pearls: 'Not starting dialysis' is itself a decision - document it formally and arrange symptom support.

subtopic_detail: Pre-renal vs intrinsic - urine sodium and FENa interpretation
stem: A 68-year-old man presents to ED with five days of vomiting, diarrhoea and reduced oral intake after a community gastroenteritis outbreak. Past history: hypertension, T2DM, IHD with previous CABG. Medications: perindopril 8 mg, indapamide 2.5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, empagliflozin 10 mg (held 48 hours ago by his GP), atorvastatin 40 mg, aspirin 100 mg. He has not taken any NSAIDs or had recent imaging contrast. On arrival: BP 96/58 mmHg lying, 78/44 mmHg sitting, HR 112, dry mucous membranes, JVP not visible, no peripheral oedema, soft non-tender abdomen, bladder not palpable. Bloods: Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.0, urea 22 mmol/L, creatinine 198 micromol/L (baseline 88 three months ago), eGFR 28, HCO3 19, lactate 2.4, glucose 9 mmol/L, CRP 12. Urinalysis: SG 1.030, no blood, no protein, no nitrites, no casts. Spot urine: sodium 8 mmol/L, urine creatinine 12 mmol/L, urine osmolality 612 mOsm/kg. Renal tract US: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, capillary refill 3 sec, JVP flat, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Urea 22, Cr 198 (baseline 88), urine Na 8, urine osm 612, FENa < 1%, US normal, no obstruction.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril, indapamide, metformin, empagliflozin (held), aspirin, atorvastatin.

lead_in: Which finding best supports a pre-renal aetiology over established acute tubular necrosis?

options.text: Postural hypotension on arrival with dry mucous membranes on examination
options.rationale: Orthostasis indicates volume depletion that explains the cause, but pre-renal physiology can progress to ATN if hypoperfusion is severe or prolonged. The discriminator between the two is tubular function on urine indices (Source: BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024).

options.text: Creatinine has more than doubled from her three-month baseline value
options.rationale: A doubling of creatinine satisfies KDIGO stage 2 AKI but does not discriminate between pre-renal and ATN; both can produce that rise (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012).

options.text: Urine sodium 8 mmol/L with FENa < 1% and urine osmolality 612 mOsm/kg
options.rationale: Intact tubules reabsorb sodium and concentrate urine when perfusion is low; urine Na < 20 mmol/L, FENa < 1% and urine osmolality > 500 mOsm/kg are characteristic of pre-renal AKI. ATN shows urine Na > 40, FENa > 2% and isosthenuric urine (~ 300) due to lost concentrating ability (Source: BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024; KDIGO AKI 2012).

options.text: Hypernatraemia (sodium 148 mmol/L) reflecting a free water deficit
options.rationale: Hypernatraemia reflects free water deficit, supporting hypovolaemia, but it is not a discriminator between pre-renal AKI and ATN - both can occur in a dehydrated patient (Source: BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024).

options.text: Absence of hydronephrosis on bedside renal tract ultrasound today
options.rationale: Absence of hydronephrosis rules out post-renal obstruction; it does not distinguish pre-renal from intrinsic causes (Source: BMJ Best Practice AKI 2024).

explanation.summary: Urine indices are the bedside discriminator between pre-renal AKI (intact tubules reabsorb Na, concentrate urine) and ATN (tubules damaged, lose Na, fail to concentrate). FENa < 1% in oliguric AKI without diuretic use favours pre-renal (Source: KDIGO 2012).
key_points: Pre-renal: urine Na < 20, FENa < 1%, U osm > 500, BUN:Cr > 20:1
ATN: urine Na > 40, FENa > 2%, U osm ~ 300 (isosthenuric), muddy brown casts
FENa unreliable on diuretics - use FEUrea (< 35% favours pre-renal)
Reverse cause: hold ACEi/diuretics/SGLT2i, replace volume, recheck
explanation.pearls: Use FEUrea instead of FENa if the patient is on a loop or thiazide diuretic.

subtopic_detail: Risk stratification and IV crystalloid
stem: A 71-year-old woman with CKD stage 3b (eGFR 38 mL/min/1.73m^2) and T2DM (HbA1c 56 mmol/mol) is scheduled for an elective coronary angiogram for stable angina. She takes perindopril, atorvastatin, aspirin, metformin and empagliflozin. Her potassium is 4.1 mmol/L. She is euvolaemic on examination, with no peripheral oedema, JVP not raised, clear chest and BP 134/78 mmHg. She has no history of decompensated heart failure. There is no recent diuretic dose change and no current sepsis. The cardiologist plans to use iso-osmolar low-volume iodinated contrast. The renal team is asked about peri-procedural prevention of contrast-associated AKI.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/78 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, SpO2 98% RA.
data_table.Examination: Euvolaemic, clear chest, no oedema, JVP not elevated.
data_table.Investigations: Cr 132 micromol/L, eGFR 38, K 4.1, HCO3 25, HbA1c 56 mmol/mol.

lead_in: Which is the most evidence-based step to reduce her risk of contrast-associated AKI?

options.text: Prophylactic IV frusemide 40 mg before contrast to maintain urine output
options.rationale: Forced diuresis with loop diuretics increases the risk of contrast-associated AKI by causing volume depletion; it is contraindicated as prophylaxis (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012).

options.text: Oral N-acetylcysteine 1200 mg twice daily on the day of and after the procedure
options.rationale: NAC was thought to reduce contrast nephropathy but the large PRESERVE trial showed no benefit over placebo; current KDIGO guidance no longer recommends it (Source: PRESERVE NEJM 2018; KDIGO AKI 2012).

options.text: IV sodium bicarbonate infusion in preference to normal saline
options.rationale: PRESERVE showed sodium bicarbonate did not outperform sodium chloride; either is acceptable but bicarbonate offers no additional benefit (Source: PRESERVE NEJM 2018).

options.text: IV isotonic sodium chloride 1 mL/kg/hour for several hours before and after the procedure
options.rationale: Peri-procedural volume expansion with isotonic crystalloid (saline or balanced) is the only intervention with consistent benefit in randomised trials (e.g. PRESERVE) for patients with eGFR < 45 receiving iodinated contrast (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012; PRESERVE trial 2018).

options.text: Withhold all antihypertensives for 48 hours before the angiogram
options.rationale: Routine cessation of antihypertensives is not advised; some clinicians hold ACEi/ARB and SGLT2i around contrast in CKD but blanket cessation of antihypertensives risks uncontrolled BP and is not the most evidence-based prevention step (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012).

explanation.summary: The mainstay of preventing contrast-associated AKI in CKD is peri-procedural IV isotonic crystalloid; NAC and bicarbonate are no longer recommended. Hold nephrotoxins (NSAIDs) and minimise contrast volume (Source: PRESERVE 2018, KDIGO 2012).
key_points: High-risk: eGFR < 45 plus diabetes; eGFR < 30 alone
Use isotonic crystalloid 1 mL/kg/h pre and post
Use lowest volume iso-osmolar contrast
Withhold metformin from procedure day until renal recovery confirmed; restart SGLT2i once stable
explanation.pearls: NAC and IV bicarbonate showed no benefit in PRESERVE - drop them from the bundle.

subtopic_detail: Nephrotic vs nephritic syndrome
stem: A 34-year-old man presents to his GP with two weeks of progressive ankle and periorbital swelling. He has gained 6 kg, noticed frothy urine and feels tired. He has no joint pains, no rashes, no recent sore throat or skin infection, and no haemoptysis. He takes no regular medications and has no allergies. Examination: BP 138/86 mmHg, HR 78, periorbital and pitting peripheral oedema to the knees, normal heart sounds, clear chest, soft abdomen with no organomegaly, no purpura. Bloods: albumin 22 g/L, total protein 52 g/L, cholesterol 8.8 mmol/L, LDL 5.4, creatinine 88 micromol/L, eGFR > 90, normal LFTs, normal FBC, complement C3 and C4 normal, ANA and ANCA negative, hepatitis B and C and HIV serology negative. Urinalysis: protein 4+, blood trace, no leucocytes or nitrites. Spot urine protein:creatinine ratio 480 mg/mmol. No active urinary sediment is reported. There is no haemoptysis or sinus disease.

data_table.Examination: Periorbital + bilateral pitting peripheral oedema; chest clear; no rash.
data_table.Investigations: Albumin 22 g/L, cholesterol 8.8 mmol/L, Cr 88, eGFR > 90, UPCR 480 mg/mmol, urinalysis protein 4+ blood trace, C3/C4 normal, ANA/ANCA/HBV/HCV/HIV negative.

lead_in: Which feature most strongly supports a nephrotic rather than a nephritic process?

options.text: Normal serum complement C3 and C4 on initial screening bloods
options.rationale: Normal complement is non-specific - membranous, minimal change and FSGS (nephrotic) and IgA nephropathy and ANCA vasculitis (nephritic) all have normal complement; this does not discriminate (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Mildly elevated blood pressure (138/86 mmHg) on examination today
options.rationale: Hypertension can occur in both nephrotic and nephritic syndromes and tends to be more prominent in nephritic disease; it does not discriminate (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Glomerulonephritis).

options.text: Trace haematuria on dipstick without dysmorphic red cells on microscopy
options.rationale: Microscopic haematuria can be seen in nephrotic-range disease (especially membranous and FSGS) but heavy haematuria with active sediment (red cell casts, dysmorphic RBCs) defines nephritic disease (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Heavy proteinuria with UPCR 480 mg/mmol and hypoalbuminaemia of 22 g/L
options.rationale: Nephrotic syndrome is defined by proteinuria > 3.5 g/24h (approximately UPCR > 300 mg/mmol), serum albumin < 30 g/L, oedema and hyperlipidaemia. UPCR 480 mg/mmol exceeds the nephrotic threshold and aligns with the hypoalbuminaemia and dyslipidaemia (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Nephrotic syndrome).

options.text: Negative ANA and ANCA serology on the secondary cause screen
options.rationale: Negative serology rules out specific secondary causes but does not differentiate between nephrotic and nephritic patterns - it only narrows the differential within each (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

explanation.summary: Nephrotic = heavy proteinuria > 3.5 g/day + hypoalbuminaemia + oedema + hyperlipidaemia. Nephritic = haematuria with active sediment (red cell casts, dysmorphic RBCs), hypertension, AKI, sub-nephrotic proteinuria (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).
key_points: UPCR > 300 mg/mmol approximates > 3.5 g/24h
Nephrotic causes (adult): membranous, FSGS, minimal change, diabetic nephropathy
Nephritic causes: IgA nephropathy, post-strep, ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM, lupus
Next step: renal biopsy + secondary cause screen
explanation.pearls: Frothy urine + periorbital oedema in an adult should prompt UPCR and albumin, not just a dipstick.

subtopic_detail: ACR/UPCR treatment threshold and ACEi/ARB role
stem: A 27-year-old previously well man is referred to nephrology after two episodes of frank haematuria coinciding with a sore throat. Renal biopsy six weeks ago confirmed IgA nephropathy with mesangial proliferation and no crescents. He has no rash, joint pain or systemic symptoms. Family history of CKD is negative. Today, after 12 weeks of lifestyle advice and observation, his BP is 138/88 mmHg, eGFR is 78 mL/min/1.73m^2 and stable, ACR is 78 mg/mmol (approximately 0.78 g/g; estimated 24-hour proteinuria 1.1 g) on first-morning samples on two occasions. Microscopic haematuria persists. He takes no regular medications. Hepatitis serology is negative. He has no contraindication to ACE inhibitors, no recent pregnancy plans (not relevant - male) and potassium is 4.2 mmol/L. He has no signs of nephrotic syndrome and complement is normal.

data_table.Examination: No oedema, no rash, no joint swelling.
data_table.Investigations: Biopsy: IgA nephropathy, no crescents. eGFR 78 (stable), ACR 78 mg/mmol (confirmed), microscopic haematuria, K 4.2.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in his management?

options.text: Continue lifestyle measures alone and recheck urine ACR in six months' time
options.rationale: His ACR is well above the 0.5 g/day (UPCR ~ 50 mg/mmol) threshold at which Australian and KDIGO guidance recommend initiating ACEi/ARB plus BP target < 120/70 mmHg in IgA nephropathy; observation alone is inadequate (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Commence high-dose IV cyclophosphamide induction immediately without further investigation
options.rationale: Cyclophosphamide is reserved for rapidly progressive (crescentic) IgA nephropathy, not for stable disease with proteinuria around 1 g/day (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Commence high-dose oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg daily as first-line agent today
options.rationale: Systemic glucocorticoids are not first-line in IgA nephropathy; they carry significant toxicity and the TESTING and STOP-IgAN trials showed limited net benefit at general doses. Steroids are considered only after maximal RAS blockade with persistent high-risk proteinuria, and increasingly replaced by targeted release budesonide (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Refer for elective tonsillectomy as the patient's definitive disease-modifying therapy
options.rationale: Tonsillectomy is used in some Japanese practice but is not supported by KDIGO or KHA-CARI as a routine intervention in IgA nephropathy outside that context (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Maximise ACE inhibitor or ARB, targeting BP < 120/70 mmHg and proteinuria < 1 g/day
options.rationale: First-line treatment of IgA nephropathy with proteinuria > 0.5 g/day is a renin-angiotensin system inhibitor titrated to the maximum tolerated dose with strict BP control (KDIGO target < 120/70). If proteinuria persists above 1 g/day after 3-6 months of optimised RAS blockade, second-line options (corticosteroids in selected cases, targeted release budesonide, SGLT2i) are considered (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021; KHA-CARI IgA nephropathy).

explanation.summary: IgA nephropathy management is built on maximally tolerated RAS blockade with a tight BP target and proteinuria goal < 1 g/day. Second-line options (SGLT2i, targeted-release budesonide, selected steroid use) are added only when proteinuria persists despite optimal first-line therapy (Source: KDIGO 2021).
key_points: Treat if proteinuria > 0.5 g/day (UPCR ~ 50 mg/mmol)
BP target < 120/70 mmHg in proteinuric glomerular disease
Target proteinuria < 1 g/day after 3-6 months
SGLT2i added for additional renoprotection; budesonide targeted-release (Nefecon) emerging
explanation.pearls: Don't reach for steroids before optimising ACEi/ARB and SGLT2i.

subtopic_detail: Renal-limited / pulmonary-renal syndrome management
stem: A 64-year-old woman is admitted with three weeks of malaise, weight loss, sinus crusting and small-volume haemoptysis, on a background of two months of arthralgia. She has no history of smoking, no exposure to silica or solvents. Examination: BP 156/92 mmHg, saturation 95% on RA, scattered fine crackles bilaterally, mild ankle oedema, palpable purpura on the lower legs. Bloods on admission: creatinine 312 micromol/L (was 84 three months ago), eGFR 14, urea 28, K 4.8, Hb 96 g/L, platelets 380, WCC 11, CRP 124, ESR 92, albumin 28 g/L. Urinalysis: blood 3+, protein 2+, red cell casts seen on phase-contrast microscopy. UPCR 180 mg/mmol. Serology: MPO-ANCA positive, PR3-ANCA negative, ANA negative, anti-GBM negative, complement C3 and C4 normal, hepatitis B/C and HIV negative. CT chest: bilateral peripheral ground-glass opacities. Renal biopsy is being arranged urgently.

data_table.Examination: Fine crackles bilaterally, palpable purpura lower legs, sinus crusting.
data_table.Investigations: Cr 312 (was 84), urea 28, K 4.8, Hb 96, CRP 124, MPO-ANCA positive, anti-GBM negative, urine red cell casts, UPCR 180 mg/mmol, CT chest peripheral GGO.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate initial induction regimen?

options.text: Oral prednisolone 1 mg/kg alone titrated to symptom control over weeks
options.rationale: Steroid monotherapy is inadequate for organ-threatening AAV with RPGN; combined induction with rituximab or cyclophosphamide is required to prevent ESKD (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: IV methylprednisolone then oral prednisolone, plus rituximab (or cyclophosphamide), with PJP prophylaxis
options.rationale: Severe organ-threatening ANCA-associated vasculitis with rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis and pulmonary involvement requires combined glucocorticoid plus rituximab (preferred in most settings, particularly women of reproductive age or where fertility preservation matters) or cyclophosphamide induction. PJP prophylaxis is standard during induction (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021; EULAR/ACR AAV).

options.text: Therapeutic plasma exchange alone as the sole intervention for induction
options.rationale: Plasma exchange is considered as an adjunct in selected severe presentations (e.g. dialysis-requiring AAV or diffuse alveolar haemorrhage), but never as monotherapy; it must be combined with steroids and an immunosuppressant (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021; PEXIVAS).

options.text: Oral mycophenolate mofetil 1 g twice daily as monotherapy for induction
options.rationale: MMF has a role in remission maintenance and in selected non-severe AAV, but not as induction monotherapy in severe organ-threatening disease (Source: EULAR/ACR AAV guidelines 2022).

options.text: Await renal biopsy result before starting any disease-modifying immunosuppression
options.rationale: MPO-ANCA-positive RPGN with peripheral GGO is treated empirically once anti-GBM has been excluded; delay risks irreversible loss of nephrons. Biopsy is arranged but should not delay induction (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

explanation.summary: Organ-threatening ANCA vasculitis requires prompt combined induction with high-dose glucocorticoid plus rituximab or cyclophosphamide, with PJP prophylaxis. Plasma exchange is selected (severe renal or pulmonary haemorrhage) per PEXIVAS data (Source: KDIGO 2021).
key_points: Rituximab non-inferior to cyclophosphamide (RAVE, RITUXVAS)
Reduced-dose steroid regimen per PEXIVAS
Plasma exchange: selected severe disease, esp dialysis or DAH
PJP prophylaxis routine during induction (TMP-SMX)
explanation.pearls: Don't wait for the biopsy if the serology, sediment and clinical picture all point to RPGN.

subtopic_detail: Discriminating TTP, HUS, aHUS
stem: A 38-year-old woman is admitted with three days of fatigue, easy bruising, and confusion. She had a vague URTI two weeks ago, denies diarrhoea, and is not pregnant (LMP one week ago). Medications: nil regular. No new drugs or supplements. Examination: T 37.8, BP 146/90, HR 104, GCS 14 (oriented to person, intermittent disorientation to place), petechiae on shins, mild jaundice, no lymphadenopathy. Bloods: Hb 78 g/L with schistocytes on blood film, platelets 18 x10^9/L, LDH 1820 U/L, haptoglobin undetectable, indirect bilirubin elevated, DCT negative, INR 1.0, APTT normal, fibrinogen 3.2 g/L, creatinine 142 micromol/L (was 70), urea 12, troponin mildly elevated, urinalysis blood 1+ protein 1+. Stool culture for Shiga toxin-producing E. coli has been sent but is not yet back. ADAMTS13 activity has been sent. She has no history of malignancy, no recent transplant or chemotherapy.

data_table.Examination: GCS 14, intermittent disorientation, petechiae, mild jaundice.
data_table.Investigations: Hb 78 + schistocytes, plt 18, LDH 1820, haptoglobin undetectable, DCT negative, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 3.2, Cr 142 (was 70), troponin mildly raised.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step before ADAMTS13 results return?

options.text: Await stool Shiga toxin PCR and ADAMTS13 activity before any TMA-specific therapy
options.rationale: Withholding treatment for TMA pending results is unsafe; untreated TTP carries > 90% mortality. Empirical plasma exchange is the standard of care when ADAMTS13 deficiency is clinically likely (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).

options.text: Transfuse 1 unit of platelets to raise the platelet count above 50 x10^9/L
options.rationale: Platelet transfusion in suspected TTP can precipitate further microvascular thrombosis and is contraindicated unless there is life-threatening bleeding or a procedure is required (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020).

options.text: Start eculizumab as the first-line therapy for presumed complement-mediated TMA
options.rationale: Eculizumab is the treatment of choice for atypical HUS (complement-mediated), suggested by predominant renal injury without severe ADAMTS13 deficiency. The clinical picture here is TTP-predominant; eculizumab is considered only if ADAMTS13 is preserved and aHUS is diagnosed (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Commence urgent therapeutic plasma exchange and high-dose corticosteroids for empirical TTP
options.rationale: MAHA + severe thrombocytopenia + neurological involvement + cardiac involvement + only modest renal dysfunction is the textbook TTP phenotype. ADAMTS13 results take time and TTP has > 90% untreated mortality, so empirical plasma exchange and steroids start immediately on clinical suspicion (Source: ISTH TTP Guideline 2020; BSH 2023).

options.text: Commence broad-spectrum IV antibiotics for presumed sepsis-related disseminated intravascular coagulation
options.rationale: DIC is unlikely with a normal INR/APTT and normal fibrinogen; the picture is non-immune MAHA + thrombocytopenia with normal coagulation, which is the haematological signature of TMA, not DIC (Source: ISTH DIC vs TMA review).

explanation.summary: TMA = MAHA + thrombocytopenia + organ injury. TTP: neuro/cardiac dominant, mild renal, ADAMTS13 < 10%, plasma exchange empirically. Typical HUS: diarrhoeal Shiga toxin (children especially). aHUS: renal-predominant, complement-mediated, treat with eculizumab (Source: KDIGO 2021).
key_points: PLASMIC score helps stratify TTP probability while awaiting ADAMTS13
Avoid platelet transfusion in suspected TTP
DIC ruled out by normal INR/APTT and fibrinogen
Drug-induced TMA (quinine, calcineurin inhibitors, gemcitabine) - withdraw the drug
explanation.pearls: Neuro signs + cardiac troponin + relatively preserved kidneys = think TTP, start PLEX.

subtopic_detail: Acute interstitial nephritis vs acute tubular necrosis
stem: A 62-year-old woman is referred to ED with a creatinine rise noted on routine bloods. Six weeks ago she was commenced on a proton pump inhibitor for reflux and three weeks ago she had a course of amoxicillin-clavulanate for sinusitis. She reports a non-pruritic maculopapular rash on her trunk over the last week, intermittent low-grade fever, and mild fatigue. She denies vomiting, diarrhoea, dehydration, NSAID use, or contrast exposure. Her usual medications are perindopril and metformin. Examination: BP 134/82, HR 84, T 37.6, mild non-blanching erythematous rash trunk and proximal limbs, no oedema, JVP not raised, clear chest. Bloods: Cr 198 micromol/L (baseline 70), eGFR 28, K 4.4, HCO3 21, eosinophils 0.8 x10^9/L (raised), CRP 24. Urinalysis: blood trace, protein 1+, leucocytes 2+, nitrites negative. Urine microscopy: white cells present, no bacteria, no red cell casts; eosinophiluria reported on Wright stain. Renal tract US: normal-sized kidneys, no hydronephrosis.

data_table.Examination: Non-pruritic maculopapular rash trunk + proximal limbs, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: Cr 198 (baseline 70), eGFR 28, peripheral eosinophils 0.8, urine WBC + eosinophiluria, no casts, US normal.
data_table.Medications: PPI (6/52), amoxicillin-clavulanate completed 1/52 ago, perindopril, metformin.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis and most appropriate next step?

options.text: Pre-renal AKI - hold the ACE inhibitor and rehydrate with IV crystalloid
options.rationale: Pre-renal AKI is supported by hypovolaemia, urine Na < 20 and concentrated urine; this patient is euvolaemic and has sterile pyuria with eosinophiluria, which is not the pre-renal pattern (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012).

options.text: Acute tubular necrosis - give IV crystalloid resuscitation and observe with serial bloods
options.rationale: ATN typically follows a hypotensive, nephrotoxic or ischaemic insult and shows muddy brown casts with urine Na > 40 and FENa > 2%; there is no haemodynamic insult here, and the rash + eosinophilia + sterile pyuria fit AIN, not ATN (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012).

options.text: Pyelonephritis - commence IV gentamicin and ceftriaxone after blood and urine cultures
options.rationale: Pyelonephritis would show nitrites or bacteriuria, flank pain, and significant systemic features; sterile pyuria with eosinophiluria and no bacteria argues against UTI, and empirical aminoglycoside risks further nephrotoxicity (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - pyelonephritis).

options.text: Rapidly progressive glomerulonephritis - urgent biopsy and pulse IV methylprednisolone
options.rationale: RPGN typically presents with hypertension, oliguria, red cell casts, dysmorphic RBCs and significant proteinuria. The sediment here is sterile pyuria with no red cell casts, which argues against a glomerular process (Source: KDIGO Glomerular Diseases 2021).

options.text: Acute interstitial nephritis - withdraw the offending drug(s) and consider corticosteroids if no improvement
options.rationale: The triad of rash, fever and eosinophilia with sterile pyuria/eosinophiluria after a new PPI and recent beta-lactam is highly suggestive of drug-induced AIN. Withdrawal of the offending drug is the cornerstone; corticosteroids are considered (typically prednisolone 0.5-1 mg/kg) if renal function does not improve within 7 days or if biopsy confirms AIN (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012; BMJ Best Practice AIN).
source_refs: BMJ Best Practice - Acute interstitial nephritis

explanation.summary: Drug-induced AIN shows AKI with sterile pyuria, eosinophiluria, often rash/fever/eosinophilia. Common culprits: PPIs, NSAIDs, beta-lactams, sulfa drugs, allopurinol. Treatment is drug withdrawal +/- steroids (Source: KDIGO AKI 2012).
key_points: Eosinophiluria has poor sensitivity but supports AIN when present
Biopsy is definitive if doubt or no improvement after drug withdrawal
Common drugs: PPIs, NSAIDs, beta-lactams, sulfa, allopurinol, mesalazine
Consider steroids if no improvement at 7 days or severe presentation
explanation.pearls: Always reconcile every drug commenced in the 8 weeks before an unexplained AKI.

sources.label: BMJ Best Practice - Acute interstitial nephritis

subtopic_detail: CK threshold, IV crystalloid, hyperkalaemia surveillance
stem: A 52-year-old man is brought to ED after being found on the floor of his home approximately 18 hours after a fall complicated by alcohol intoxication. He has no head injury but is unable to bear weight on the left side due to pain. Past history: hypertension on perindopril, gout on allopurinol, harmful alcohol use. Examination: GCS 15, BP 132/82, HR 104, RR 18, T 36.8, SpO2 97% RA, dependent oedema and skin tenderness over the left thigh and calf, dark tea-coloured urine when catheterised. Bloods on arrival: Cr 168 micromol/L (baseline 88), K 5.6, HCO3 19, Na 138, urea 12, glucose 6.0, CK 84,000 U/L, troponin not elevated, lactate 2.6, AST 380, ALT 110. Urinalysis: dipstick positive for blood 4+, but microscopy shows no red blood cells. ECG: sinus tachycardia, no peaked T waves. He has not taken statins recently and no other myotoxic drug exposure is identified.

data_table.Examination: Tender left thigh + calf, dark urine, dependent oedema.
data_table.Investigations: CK 84,000 U/L, Cr 168 (baseline 88), K 5.6, HCO3 19, urine dipstick blood 4+ but no RBC on micro, ECG sinus tachy.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: IV frusemide 80 mg bolus to force diuresis through depleted tubules
options.rationale: Forced diuresis with loop diuretics is not supported and may worsen volume status and AKI; the goal is to expand intravascular volume, not to force urine through depleted tubules (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).

options.text: Urgent haemodialysis via a temporary vascath to clear circulating myoglobin
options.rationale: Myoglobin is poorly cleared by haemodialysis; dialysis is reserved for the standard AKI indications (refractory hyperkalaemia, severe acidosis, fluid overload, uraemia) rather than for myoglobin removal per se (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis).

options.text: Aggressive IV isotonic crystalloid (1-2 L/h) titrated to urine output 200-300 mL/h
options.rationale: The mainstay of rhabdomyolysis management is large-volume isotonic crystalloid to maintain urine flow and prevent pigment-cast nephropathy, plus aggressive monitoring and treatment of hyperkalaemia. Targeted urine output is 200-300 mL/h. Outcomes correlate with how early fluids are started (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis; Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).

options.text: IV mannitol 1 g/kg bolus to scavenge oxygen free radicals and force diuresis
options.rationale: Routine mannitol is not supported by evidence and risks volume overload, hyperosmolarity and acute kidney injury in its own right; isotonic crystalloid is the standard (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis).

options.text: Restrict fluids and observe given a normal blood pressure on arrival
options.rationale: Fluid restriction risks pigment cast nephropathy and irreversible AKI; even with a normal BP, intravascular volume is contracted after rhabdomyolysis and aggressive crystalloid is needed (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Rhabdomyolysis).

explanation.summary: Rhabdomyolysis is defined as CK > 5x ULN (commonly > 1000 U/L) with myoglobinuria risking pigment-cast AKI. Management is large-volume isotonic crystalloid, urine output target 200-300 mL/h, surveillance for hyperkalaemia, hypocalcaemia, acidosis and compartment syndrome (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).
key_points: Dipstick positive for blood + no RBC on micro = myoglobinuria
Watch for hyperkalaemia early; hypocalcaemia from Ca-PO4 precipitation
Hold/avoid nephrotoxins (ACEi, NSAIDs, contrast)
Mannitol and bicarbonate not routinely recommended
explanation.pearls: Compartment syndrome can be both cause and consequence - re-examine swollen limbs.

subtopic_detail: ECG-driven escalation ladder
stem: A 74-year-old man with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73m^2) and heart failure attends ED with malaise and palpitations. Medications: irbesartan 300 mg, spironolactone 25 mg, frusemide 40 mg, metoprolol, atorvastatin. He was started on trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 5 days ago for a UTI by his GP. Examination: BP 138/82, HR 52 with a regular pulse, no syncope, alert. Bloods on arrival: K 7.3 mmol/L, Na 134, Cr 248 micromol/L (was 198 last month), HCO3 19, glucose 6.4, Hb 110. ECG shows sinus bradycardia with prolonged PR, broad QRS approximately 140 ms, and tall peaked T waves in V2-V5.

data_table.Examination: Alert, well-perfused, mild peripheral oedema.
data_table.Investigations: K 7.3, Cr 248 (was 198), HCO3 19, ECG: peaked T waves, broad QRS 140 ms, prolonged PR.

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate immediate intervention?

options.text: IV calcium gluconate 10% 10 mL over 2-3 minutes to stabilise the myocardium
options.rationale: ECG changes (peaked T waves, prolonged PR, broad QRS) with hyperkalaemia require immediate myocardial membrane stabilisation. IV calcium does not lower potassium but rapidly protects the heart while shift and removal strategies are arranged (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology - hyperkalaemia; UK Renal Association 2023).

options.text: IV actrapid insulin 10 units with 50 mL of 50% dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly
options.rationale: Insulin-dextrose is the next step but should follow calcium when there are ECG changes; shifting potassium without membrane stabilisation does not protect against arrhythmia in the short window before the shift takes effect (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).

options.text: Nebulised salbutamol 10-20 mg via mask to shift potassium intracellularly
options.rationale: Salbutamol shifts potassium intracellularly via beta-2 stimulation but is slower and less reliable than insulin-dextrose; like other shift therapies it does not precede membrane stabilisation when ECG changes are present (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).

options.text: Oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate (resonium) 30 g as an enteral cation exchanger
options.rationale: Cation exchangers act over hours and do not protect against arrhythmia; they remove potassium from the body rather than stabilising myocardium or shifting potassium acutely (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).

options.text: Urgent in-centre haemodialysis via a temporary internal jugular vascath
options.rationale: Dialysis is the definitive removal therapy in refractory or severe cases, but it takes time to set up; calcium and shift therapy come first, with dialysis arranged in parallel if there is no response (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia management ladder: calcium (stabilise myocardium) -> insulin-dextrose + salbutamol (shift) -> diuretics, cation exchangers, dialysis (remove). ECG changes mandate calcium first regardless of the numeric K (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Nephrology).
key_points: Common culprits in CKD: ACEi/ARB, spironolactone, TMP-SMX (acts like K-sparing diuretic)
Calcium chloride 10% 5-10 mL via large vein if no central line for gluconate
Repeat ECG and K 30-60 min after intervention
Identify and treat cause; stop offending drugs
explanation.pearls: TMP-SMX raises potassium by trimethoprim blocking the ENaC channel - common, overlooked.

subtopic_detail: Magnesium repletion priority before potassium
stem: A 68-year-old woman is admitted from a residential aged care facility with two days of vomiting and reduced oral intake. She has long-standing heart failure on frusemide 80 mg daily and metolazone 2.5 mg three times weekly. Other medications include pantoprazole for several years, digoxin, perindopril and atorvastatin. Examination: BP 122/72, HR 96 with frequent ectopics, dry mucous membranes, no oedema. Bloods on admission: K 2.6 mmol/L, Mg 0.55 mmol/L, Ca 2.30 corrected, Na 132, HCO3 32, Cr 142, eGFR 38, digoxin level pending. ECG: sinus rhythm 96, prominent U waves, occasional ventricular ectopics, QT prolonged at 480 ms. She has received 40 mmol of oral KCl slow-release tablets six hours ago at the facility but the potassium has not improved on the repeat venous sample. She is not on insulin and has no diabetic ketoacidosis.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, no oedema.
data_table.Investigations: K 2.6, Mg 0.55, Na 132, HCO3 32, Cr 142, ECG: U waves, QT 480 ms, ectopics.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step in correcting her electrolytes?

options.text: Start an IV potassium infusion at 40 mmol/hour via peripheral cannula
options.rationale: Peripheral IV potassium should not exceed 10 mmol/h (max 20 mmol/h in a closely monitored area); 40 mmol/h risks chemical phlebitis, cardiac arrhythmias and is unsafe peripherally (Source: AMH - potassium chloride).

options.text: Increase oral potassium chloride slow-release tablets to 80 mmol immediately
options.rationale: More oral potassium without addressing magnesium will not correct the deficit because urinary potassium losses continue while magnesium is low; large oral doses also risk GI side effects (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).

options.text: Replace magnesium intravenously (e.g. magnesium sulfate 10 mmol) alongside potassium replacement
options.rationale: Hypomagnesaemia drives renal potassium wasting through ROMK channel disinhibition; correcting potassium without correcting magnesium fails. IV magnesium repletion alongside potassium is the standard step. Loop diuretics, PPIs and metolazone are all magnesium-wasting (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes; AMH).

options.text: Stop pantoprazole and recheck potassium in 48 hours
options.rationale: Long-term PPI use contributes to hypomagnesaemia and should be reviewed, but stopping it does not address the current life-threatening hypokalaemia with ECG changes; acute replacement is required now (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).

options.text: Give IV sodium bicarbonate for the alkalosis
options.rationale: Bicarbonate would worsen the contraction alkalosis and drive potassium further intracellularly, deepening the hypokalaemia (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).

explanation.summary: Hypokalaemia refractory to potassium replacement should prompt a magnesium check. Hypomagnesaemia activates ROMK and accelerates urinary K loss; co-correction is required (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Electrolytes).
key_points: Common offenders: loops + thiazides, PPIs, alcoholism, diarrhoea
Peripheral IV K+ max 10 mmol/h (cardiac monitored areas: 20 mmol/h)
Hold digoxin if level high or arrhythmias (hypokalaemia potentiates digoxin toxicity)
Always check Mg + Ca with K disturbances
explanation.pearls: K won't come up while Mg is low - replace Mg first or simultaneously.

subtopic_detail: Rate of correction and osmotic demyelination prevention
stem: A 62-year-old woman with a history of harmful alcohol use is brought to ED after being found drowsy. She has eaten poorly for two weeks. Examination: GCS 11, BP 110/70, HR 96, RR 14, T 36.5, weight 58 kg, mucous membranes moist, no peripheral oedema, no focal neurology, no asterixis. Bloods on arrival: Na 108 mmol/L, K 3.0, urea 2.4 mmol/L, glucose 4.2, serum osmolality 232 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 460 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 38 mmol/L, TSH normal, random cortisol normal. She is not on diuretics; her only regular medication is thiamine (irregularly taken). She has had one self-limited generalised tonic-clonic seizure since arrival. No history of CCF, cirrhosis or recent surgery; no SSRI, no carbamazepine.

data_table.Vital signs: GCS 11, BP 110/70, HR 96, T 36.5, weight 58 kg.
data_table.Examination: Mucous membranes moist, no oedema, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Na 108, K 3.0, urea 2.4, glucose 4.2, serum osm 232, urine osm 460, urine Na 38, TSH and cortisol normal.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: IV hypertonic saline (3%) 150 mL boluses, capping the 24-hour sodium rise at 8-10 mmol/L
options.rationale: Severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (seizure, GCS 11) warrants rapid initial correction with 100-150 mL hypertonic saline boluses to relieve cerebral oedema, but the 24-hour rise must be capped at 8-10 mmol/L (and 18 mmol/L over 48 h) to prevent osmotic demyelination syndrome. This is doubly important here given malnutrition and harmful alcohol use, which are additional ODS risk factors (Source: European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014; ANZICS / Australian intensivist statements).

options.text: IV 0.9% sodium chloride at 250 mL/h with hourly sodium measurement
options.rationale: Isotonic saline is too slow to address acute cerebral oedema with seizures and may paradoxically worsen hyponatraemia in SIADH (de-saltation effect). Hypertonic saline is the agent of choice for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Free water restriction to 800 mL/day as the sole acute intervention
options.rationale: Water restriction is appropriate for asymptomatic chronic SIADH but is inadequate for severe symptomatic hyponatraemia with seizure; rapid initial correction with hypertonic saline is needed first (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: IV tolvaptan 15 mg orally to block V2 receptors and aquarese free water
options.rationale: Vaptans risk over-rapid correction and ODS, especially in alcoholic malnourished patients; they are not recommended in severe symptomatic hyponatraemia at presentation (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Allow spontaneous correction by treating the underlying cause aggressively over 24 hours
options.rationale: Uncontrolled correction is the precise mechanism that produces ODS; an explicit cap on the 24-hour rise (8-10 mmol/L) is mandatory in high-risk patients (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

explanation.summary: Symptomatic severe hyponatraemia: hypertonic saline boluses to gain 4-6 mmol/L quickly for symptom relief, then cap the 24-h rise at 8-10 mmol/L (10-12 in low-risk, but lower in alcohol/malnutrition/liver disease) and 18 mmol/L over 48 h. If over-correction, re-lower with 5% dextrose and consider desmopressin (Source: European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014).
key_points: Symptom severity (seizure, GCS drop) drives use of hypertonic saline, not absolute Na
ODS risk highest if Na < 105, alcohol, malnutrition, hypokalaemia, liver disease
Replace K (low here) carefully - K replacement raises Na as well
Reverse over-correction with 5% dextrose +/- desmopressin
explanation.pearls: Replace K - it counts as a corrected osmole and contributes to over-rapid Na rise.

subtopic_detail: Outpatient SIADH workup - drug and paraneoplastic causes
stem: A 72-year-old man is referred from his GP after an asymptomatic sodium of 124 mmol/L on a routine pre-operative panel. He has a 50-pack-year smoking history and a chronic cough for three months with mild weight loss. Medications: amlodipine, atorvastatin, sertraline (started six months ago) and carbamazepine (long-standing for trigeminal neuralgia). He drinks 2 cups of tea per day, denies polydipsia, and is otherwise well. Examination: BP 132/78 lying and standing, HR 76, JVP not raised, mucous membranes moist, no peripheral oedema, normal cardiovascular and respiratory exam. Bloods: Na 124, K 3.9, urea 3.0, creatinine 76 micromol/L, eGFR > 90, glucose 5.0, serum osmolality 258 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 412 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 48 mmol/L, TSH 1.8 mIU/L, random cortisol 380 nmol/L. Chest X-ray is requested.

data_table.Examination: Euvolaemic, no oedema, no postural BP drop.
data_table.Investigations: Na 124, serum osm 258, urine osm 412, urine Na 48, TSH 1.8, cortisol 380.

lead_in: Which is the most likely cause of his hyponatraemia and the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Adrenal insufficiency causing hyponatraemia - commence empirical IV hydrocortisone today
options.rationale: Random cortisol 380 nmol/L argues against primary adrenal insufficiency (consider AM cortisol and Synacthen test if uncertain); empirical glucocorticoid is not the immediate step (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Hypovolaemic hyponatraemia from poor oral intake - start oral rehydration and recheck
options.rationale: He is clinically euvolaemic with no postural drop and a urine Na of 48 mmol/L. In hypovolaemic hyponatraemia the kidney avidly reabsorbs sodium, producing urine Na < 20 mmol/L (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Primary polydipsia driving hyponatraemia - restrict fluids to 1 L/day at home
options.rationale: Primary polydipsia produces dilute urine (urine osmolality < 100 mOsm/kg) as the kidney suppresses ADH. His urine osmolality of 412 is inappropriately concentrated (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Hypothyroidism causing hyponatraemia - commence levothyroxine and recheck in 6 weeks
options.rationale: TSH is 1.8 mIU/L (within reference). Hypothyroidism is a recognised cause of euvolaemic hyponatraemia but is ruled out here (Source: European hyponatraemia clinical practice guideline 2014).

options.text: Drug-induced SIADH (SSRI/carbamazepine) - review medications and arrange a chest CT
options.rationale: Euvolaemic hyponatraemia with serum osmolality < 275, urine osmolality > 100 and urine sodium > 30 with normal thyroid and adrenal axes meets SIADH criteria. SSRIs and carbamazepine are common precipitants; the smoking history and chronic cough also raise concern for paraneoplastic SIADH from small cell lung cancer, mandating chest imaging (Source: European hyponatraemia guidelines 2014; eTG endocrinology).

explanation.summary: SIADH diagnosis requires euvolaemic, hypotonic hyponatraemia with inappropriate urine concentration and natriuresis, and excluded thyroid and adrenal causes. Common causes: drugs (SSRIs, carbamazepine, antipsychotics), CNS pathology, malignancy (particularly SCLC), pulmonary disease, postoperative (Source: eTG endocrinology).
key_points: Drug-induced SIADH: SSRIs, carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine, antipsychotics, vincristine, MDMA
Paraneoplastic SIADH: SCLC, head-and-neck, mesothelioma
First-line treatment of chronic asymptomatic SIADH: fluid restriction to 800-1000 mL/day
Second-line: salt tablets +/- loop diuretic; vaptans only in selected cases
explanation.pearls: Smoker + chronic cough + asymptomatic hyponatraemia = chest CT, don't just stop the SSRI.

subtopic_detail: Central vs nephrogenic - water deprivation + desmopressin
stem: A 45-year-old woman is referred to endocrine clinic with three months of polyuria and polydipsia (estimated 6-7 L/day), worse at night. She has bipolar disorder treated with lithium 750 mg daily for 12 years and is otherwise well. Medications: lithium, escitalopram. Examination: BP 116/72, HR 78, weight 64 kg, no postural drop, mucous membranes slightly dry, no focal neurology. Bloods: Na 148 mmol/L, K 4.2, urea 5.0, creatinine 92 micromol/L, eGFR 64, calcium corrected 2.34 mmol/L, glucose 5.2, HbA1c 38 mmol/mol, TSH 1.6, lithium level 0.7 mmol/L (therapeutic). Plasma osmolality 308 mOsm/kg, urine osmolality 198 mOsm/kg, urine specific gravity 1.005. A water deprivation test is performed: after 6 hours her urine osmolality remains 220 mOsm/kg, then desmopressin 2 microgram is given subcutaneously and urine osmolality rises only to 245 mOsm/kg.

data_table.Examination: Slightly dry mucous membranes, no focal neurology.
data_table.Investigations: Na 148, plasma osm 308, urine osm 198, water deprivation: urine osm 220 -> 245 after dDAVP, lithium 0.7, Ca 2.34.

lead_in: Which is the most likely diagnosis?

options.text: Central diabetes insipidus due to posterior pituitary ADH deficiency
options.rationale: Central DI shows a robust rise in urine osmolality (> 50%, often more than doubling) after exogenous desmopressin because the kidney responds normally. A minimal rise to 245 here excludes central DI (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Diabetes insipidus).

options.text: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, most likely lithium-induced collecting duct injury
options.rationale: Hypotonic polyuria with hypernatraemia (Na 148), persistently dilute urine after water deprivation (220 mOsm/kg) and negligible response to exogenous desmopressin (rise < 50% to 245) localises the defect to the collecting duct. Lithium is the most common drug cause of nephrogenic DI (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Diabetes insipidus; eTG endocrinology).

options.text: Primary polydipsia with secondary suppression of endogenous ADH
options.rationale: Primary polydipsia produces a low or normal serum sodium and the urine concentrates appropriately during water deprivation; a sodium of 148 with persistently dilute urine after deprivation argues against this (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Diabetes insipidus).

options.text: Osmotic diuresis from undiagnosed diabetes mellitus or glycosuria
options.rationale: HbA1c 38 mmol/mol and glucose 5.2 mmol/L exclude diabetes; osmotic diuresis would also produce a higher urine osmolality (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Diabetes insipidus).

options.text: Chronic kidney disease producing a generalised urinary concentration defect
options.rationale: Generalised CKD concentration defects rarely produce frank hypotonic polyuria with hypernatraemia; her eGFR is 64, and the pattern fits nephrogenic DI rather than a non-specific concentration defect (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Diabetes insipidus).

explanation.summary: DI is characterised by hypotonic polyuria. Central DI: dDAVP normalises urine osmolality. Nephrogenic DI: negligible response to dDAVP. Lithium is the most common acquired nephrogenic DI cause, often with reduced eGFR and tubulointerstitial damage (Source: BMJ Best Practice - DI).
key_points: Plasma osm > 295 + urine osm < 300 = DI
dDAVP test discriminates central vs nephrogenic
Lithium nephrogenic DI: dose-related but can persist after cessation
Treatment of lithium NDI: review need for lithium, amiloride, thiazide (paradoxically effective), low-salt diet
explanation.pearls: Always check lithium level and reconcile psychotropic meds in polyuria + hypernatraemia.

subtopic_detail: Malignancy-related vs primary hyperparathyroidism
stem: A 67-year-old man presents with two weeks of fatigue, anorexia, constipation and confusion. He has a history of 6 months of progressive low back pain attributed to musculoskeletal strain and a 4 kg unintentional weight loss. Examination: GCS 14 (oriented to person and place, not time), BP 134/78 lying, 110/68 standing, HR 100, dry mucous membranes, mild tenderness over lumbar spine, otherwise unremarkable. Bloods on admission: corrected calcium 3.42 mmol/L, phosphate 0.65 mmol/L (low), albumin 32 g/L, creatinine 152 micromol/L (baseline 84), eGFR 38, ESR 92, total protein 96 g/L with reversed albumin:globulin ratio, Hb 92 g/L, normal WCC and platelets, 25-OH vitamin D 62 nmol/L. PTH (intact) is 0.6 pmol/L (low). Urine dipstick: protein 2+. Skeletal survey shows lytic lesions in the lumbar spine.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 134/78 lying / 110/68 standing, HR 100, T 36.9, GCS 14.
data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, lumbar spine tenderness.
data_table.Investigations: Corrected Ca 3.42, phosphate 0.65, PTH 0.6 (low), Cr 152, ESR 92, Hb 92, total protein 96 with reversed A:G, urine protein 2+, lytic L-spine lesions.

lead_in: Which is the most likely cause of his hypercalcaemia?

options.text: Vitamin D toxicity
options.rationale: Vitamin D toxicity raises both calcium and phosphate (gut absorption of both) and shows a low PTH with a high 25-OH vitamin D; his 25-OH vitamin D is 62 nmol/L which is unremarkable (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Primary hyperparathyroidism
options.rationale: Primary hyperparathyroidism is the commonest outpatient cause of hypercalcaemia but features an inappropriately normal or elevated PTH; a PTH of 0.6 pmol/L (suppressed) rules it out in this context (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Multiple myeloma
options.rationale: The pattern of suppressed PTH + lytic bone lesions + anaemia + reversed albumin:globulin ratio + renal impairment + high ESR is the textbook CRAB picture of myeloma. PTH-independent hypercalcaemia mandates a malignancy workup including SPEP, free light chains, urine Bence-Jones and skeletal imaging (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia; eviQ Myeloma).

options.text: Granulomatous disease (sarcoidosis)
options.rationale: Granulomatous hypercalcaemia is mediated by extra-renal 1-alpha-hydroxylase producing 1,25-OH vitamin D; calcium is typically modest, PTH is suppressed and lytic lesions are not the picture (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).

options.text: Thiazide-induced hypercalcaemia
options.rationale: Thiazides modestly elevate calcium by enhanced distal tubular reabsorption and usually unmask underlying primary hyperparathyroidism, with non-suppressed PTH; severe hypercalcaemia and lytic lesions are not the pattern (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).

explanation.summary: Hypercalcaemia workup divides PTH-mediated (PTH non-suppressed) from PTH-independent (PTH suppressed). Suppressed PTH points to malignancy (myeloma, PTHrP from squamous tumours, lytic bone disease), vitamin D excess, granulomas, immobilisation, milk-alkali (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Hypercalcaemia).
key_points: Confirm corrected calcium - correct for albumin
PTH-mediated: primary, tertiary hyperparathyroidism, lithium, FHH
PTH-independent: malignancy (PTHrP, lytic, 1,25-OH-D producing lymphoma), granulomas, vit D, thyrotoxicosis
Acute Rx: IV crystalloid + zoledronic acid 4 mg (caution if eGFR < 30) or denosumab
explanation.pearls: Suppressed PTH + lytic lesions + ESR > 90 = think myeloma until proven otherwise.

subtopic_detail: Mixed disorder with delta-delta in DKA
stem: A 24-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes is brought to ED with vomiting for two days and reduced consciousness. She admits to skipping her basal insulin. Examination: GCS 13, BP 102/64, HR 124, RR 34 with deep Kussmaul breathing, mucous membranes very dry, ketotic breath, abdomen mildly tender. Bloods: Na 132 mmol/L, K 5.2 mmol/L, Cl 88 mmol/L, HCO3 6 mmol/L, urea 14, creatinine 124 micromol/L, glucose 32 mmol/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate 7.8 mmol/L. ABG: pH 7.06, pCO2 16 mmHg, pO2 110 mmHg on 2 L/min O2, HCO3 6 mmol/L, BE -22, lactate 1.8. Albumin 36 g/L. She has no history of salicylate use, no ingestion of methanol or ethylene glycol, and no toxidromic features.

data_table.Examination: Dry mucous membranes, ketotic breath.
data_table.Investigations: Na 132, K 5.2, Cl 88, HCO3 6, glucose 32, beta-OHB 7.8, pH 7.06, pCO2 16, lactate 1.8, alb 36.

lead_in: Which is the most accurate description of her acid-base disorder?

options.text: High anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation and concurrent metabolic alkalosis
options.rationale: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3) = 132 - (88 + 6) = 38. Delta anion gap = 38 - 12 = 26. Delta HCO3 = 24 - 6 = 18. Delta-delta = 26/18 = approximately 1.4 - this is just above 1, but in DKA delta ratios commonly drop towards 1 when sodium loss/dilution accompanies ketoacidosis. With sustained vomiting adding bicarbonate-rich gastric losses (proton loss), the bicarbonate is being pulled upward by a concurrent metabolic alkalosis. Winters' formula predicts expected pCO2 = (1.5 x 6) + 8 = 17 mmHg; measured 16 is within +/- 2 so respiratory compensation is appropriate (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base; UpToDate Acid-base).

options.text: Pure high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate Kussmaul respiratory compensation
options.rationale: If the disorder were pure HAGMA, the fall in bicarbonate should equal the rise in anion gap (delta ratio approximately 1). Two days of vomiting would predict a delta ratio > 1 because gastric H+ losses tend to elevate bicarbonate relative to the gap rise (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

options.text: Mixed high anion gap and normal anion gap metabolic acidosis (delta ratio < 1)
options.rationale: A delta ratio below 1 implies an additional non-anion-gap acidosis (e.g. diarrhoea, RTA); the calculated delta ratio here is above 1, not below 1 (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

options.text: Primary respiratory alkalosis with secondary metabolic compensation through bicarbonate loss
options.rationale: The primary disorder is metabolic with a pH of 7.06; the low pCO2 of 16 is compensatory hyperventilation (Kussmaul) for the acidosis, not a primary respiratory alkalosis (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

options.text: Normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with a primary respiratory alkalosis overlay
options.rationale: The anion gap is 38, well above the normal range (8-12), so this is a high anion gap acidosis. NAGMA with respiratory alkalosis is the wrong characterisation (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base interpretation).

explanation.summary: Stepwise acid-base: pH then primary disorder, then anion gap, then delta-delta to detect mixed disorders, then check Winters' for respiratory compensation. Albumin-correct the gap if low (add 2.5 per 10 g/L below 40) (Source: BMJ Best Practice - Acid-base).
key_points: Anion gap = Na - (Cl + HCO3); normal 8-12 in most labs
Delta ratio < 1: HAGMA + NAGMA; 1-2: pure HAGMA; > 2: HAGMA + metabolic alkalosis
Winters' formula: expected pCO2 = 1.5 x HCO3 + 8 (+/- 2)
Common DKA mixed pictures: vomiting (alkalosis), pre-existing CKD (NAGMA), sepsis (lactic)
explanation.pearls: Always do delta-delta in DKA - the bedside picture of 'just DKA' often hides another driver.

subtopic_detail: Drug dose adjustment and avoidance in CKD
stem: A 78-year-old woman with CKD stage 4 (eGFR 24 mL/min/1.73m^2) is reviewed in clinic. Her medications are perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gabapentin 600 mg three times a day for painful diabetic neuropathy, morphine MR 30 mg twice a day for chronic back pain, ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD (day 2) started by her GP for an uncomplicated UTI, and apixaban 5 mg BD for atrial fibrillation (CHA2DS2-VA 4). She reports increasing drowsiness, gait instability and one fall yesterday. There are no overtly focal neurological signs and no head injury. BP 132/76, HR 76. Bloods today: Cr 220 micromol/L (was 195 last month), eGFR 24, K 4.8, HCO3 21, INR 1.1.

data_table.Examination: Drowsy, ataxic gait, no focal neurology.
data_table.Medications: Perindopril 5 mg, metformin 1 g BD, gabapentin 600 mg TDS, morphine MR 30 mg BD, ciprofloxacin 500 mg BD (day 2), apixaban 5 mg BD.

lead_in: Which medication change is most urgently required given her renal function?

options.text: Reduce gabapentin and switch morphine to a less renally-cleared opioid such as buprenorphine
options.rationale: Gabapentin and morphine (via morphine-6-glucuronide) are both predominantly renally cleared and accumulate at low eGFR, producing sedation, ataxia and falls. Australian Medicines Handbook recommends dose reduction or alternative agents at eGFR < 30 - gabapentin reduced and titrated, and morphine substituted with an opioid such as buprenorphine or fentanyl that is less dependent on renal clearance (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment; Therapeutic Guidelines: Palliative care).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment
Therapeutic Guidelines: Palliative care - opioids in renal impairment

options.text: Stop metformin permanently today because of the lactic acidosis risk at this eGFR
options.rationale: AMH and ADS guidance allow metformin to be continued with dose reduction (typically maximum 1 g/day) at eGFR 30-45 and to be stopped or used with caution below 30; her eGFR is 24, so dose review is reasonable but the urgent driver of harm right now is opioid + gabapentin neurotoxicity (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment

options.text: Switch apixaban to warfarin titrated to an INR target of 2.0 to 3.0
options.rationale: Apixaban can be used in CKD stage 4 with a dose reduction to 2.5 mg BD if any two of age >= 80, weight <= 60 kg or creatinine >= 133 micromol/L are present; warfarin in elderly CKD has worse outcomes than DOACs in most cohorts (Source: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - apixaban).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment

options.text: Stop ciprofloxacin and switch the UTI cover to oral nitrofurantoin instead
options.rationale: Nitrofurantoin is contraindicated at eGFR < 45 because of inadequate urinary concentrations and increased systemic toxicity; ciprofloxacin requires dose reduction but is not the most urgent change (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - UTI; AMH).
source_refs: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment

options.text: Stop perindopril today given the small rise in creatinine over a month
options.rationale: A creatinine rise of < 30% on ACEi is acceptable; with K 4.8 and BP control, perindopril should be continued unless creatinine rises > 30% from baseline or potassium > 6.0 mmol/L (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Cardiovascular).

explanation.summary: In CKD, drug clearance, free fraction and active metabolite accumulation change. Highest-yield Y4 drugs to know: metformin, gabapentin/pregabalin, opioids (morphine, codeine), nitrofurantoin, digoxin, DOACs, ACEi/ARB, NSAIDs (avoid). Always cross-check with AMH (Source: AMH 2024).
key_points: Avoid NSAIDs in CKD stage 3b and worse
Avoid nitrofurantoin at eGFR < 45
Morphine accumulates via M6G; safer opioids in CKD: buprenorphine, fentanyl
Gabapentin/pregabalin dose-reduce based on CrCl
Apixaban: 2.5 mg BD if 2 of 3 (age >= 80, weight <= 60, Cr >= 133)
explanation.pearls: Drowsy + ataxic + falling on multiple renally-cleared CNS drugs is a prescribing emergency, not 'old age'.

sources.label: Australian Medicines Handbook 2024 - prescribing in renal impairment
Therapeutic Guidelines: Palliative care - opioids in renal impairment
